coredata.dc:title: Adsorption of toremifene on carboxylic acid functionalized N/B-doped coronene: DFT, AIM, solvation, docking and MD analyses

Mary, Y. Sheena

Mondal, Asmita
Acharjee, Nivedita
Rajendran Nair, Deepthi S.
coredata.dc:description: The adsorption of toremifene (TOR) on coronene (GG) and carboxylic acid-functionalized coronene (COOHGG) and doped with boron/nitrogen (BBGG/NNGG) has been researched using DFT and TD-DFT simulations. The chemical properties, AIM, and SERS were all considered when conducting this investigation. Intermolecular interactions emphasizing on reactive zones result from graphene acting as an electron acceptor and nucleophilic part of TOR acting as an electron-donor. All of the TOR-complexes had negative Gibbs free energy changes due to TOR adsorption. Enthalpy modifications also differ greatly across the doped complexes (-26.39 to −86.97 kJ/mol lin air and −65.39 to −86.97 kJ/mol in water) and GG complex (-2.55 kJ/mol in air and −40.58 kJ/mol in water). Our results show that the TOR with BBGG doped complex, with a binding energy of −55.73 kJ/mol in air, while in water adsorption with NNGG gives −90.92 kJ/mol, are the most stable complexes among the various systems under study. The GG complex has the lowest energy, followed by other doped complexes. After adsorption multiple modes of TOR have higher Raman intensities, and this is because of an amplification process brought on by the SERS effect. The presence of substituted COOHGG, BBGG and NNGG cages has a higher tendency to interact with the drug molecule than GG. From MD simulations, TOR had van der Waals energy of −147.293+/–22.370 kJ/mol, an electrostatic energy of −53.749 +/–33.825 kJ/mol, polar solvation energy of 112.531+/−30.651 kJ/mol, and a binding energy of −70.130+/–23.290 kJ/mol.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The adsorption of toremifene (TOR) on coronene (GG) and carboxylic acid-functionalized coronene (COOHGG) and doped with boron/nitrogen (BBGG/NNGG) has been researched using DFT and TD-DFT simulations. The chemical properties, AIM, and SERS were all considered when conducting this investigation. Intermolecular interactions emphasizing on reactive zones result from graphene acting as an electron acceptor and nucleophilic part of TOR acting as an electron-donor. All of the TOR-complexes had negative Gibbs free energy changes due to TOR adsorption. Enthalpy modifications also differ greatly across the doped complexes (-26.39 to −86.97 kJ/mol lin air and −65.39 to −86.97 kJ/mol in water) and GG complex (-2.55 kJ/mol in air and −40.58 kJ/mol in water). Our results show that the TOR with BBGG doped complex, with a binding energy of −55.73 kJ/mol in air, while in water adsorption with NNGG gives −90.92 kJ/mol, are the most stable complexes among the various systems under study. The GG complex has the lowest energy, followed by other doped complexes. After adsorption multiple modes of TOR have higher Raman intensities, and this is because of an amplification process brought on by the SERS effect. The presence of substituted COOHGG, BBGG and NNGG cages has a higher tendency to interact with the drug molecule than GG. From MD simulations, TOR had van der Waals energy of −147.293+/–22.370 kJ/mol, an electrostatic energy of −53.749 +/–33.825 kJ/mol, polar solvation energy of 112.531+/−30.651 kJ/mol, and a binding energy of −70.130+/–23.290 kJ/mol.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and its derivatives have received the most significant attention in recent years as metal-free-electrocatalytics [1]. Since the widespread commercialization of metal catalysts has been hampered by their manufacture and corrosion instability, a variety of it has been referred to as electrocatalytics for degrading pollutants [2]. Even though chemotherapy is a commonly used cancer treatment, traditional chemotherapeutic medicines have substantial adverse effects when administered to patients [3]. Numerous studies have been reported to seek effective drug delivery methods to deliver chemotherapy drugs to tumor tissues [4,5]. Recently, numerous nano-scale drug carriers have been used to deliver drugs to cancer patients in a tailored manner [6]. In particular, as drug transporters, nano-sized carbon allotropes have demonstrated potential in nano-medicine [7]. Graphene, particularly doped, has recently been used to create exciting fluorescent probes with unusual qualities, such as broad band absorption, strong stability, biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity [8–11].

Due to their effect on performance in treatment facilities as aquatic contaminants, pharmaceutical and personal care goods have raised a lot of worry [12]. Therefore, using efficient methods is required to reduce the amount of pollutants in aquatic habitats. Graphene and its derivatives have received the most scientific attention among these materials [13]. Investigations suggest that N/B doping, which can take the place of carbon atoms and interact with lattice, can be utilized to modify the electrical characteristics [14–17]. Nitrogen has been used to doping of nano materials due to its atomic size interacting with carbon [18]. Graphene with substituted N/B/F/P can alter the band gap and offer it new features [19–24]. B-doped graphene has a lot of holes, which increases carrier concentration and in turn, conductivity [25]. Boron is appropriate having low electronegativity than N and results in p-type conductivity in graphene [26–29]. Montejo-Alvaro et al., reported stability of Pd clusters with doped graphene and sensitivity against O adsorption [30]. Graphene with nitrogen glows blue instead of green like its N-free version [31]. Juang et al., discovered that B-graphene produces luminous green and red emissions under blue light illumination [32]. There have been several studies on adsorption of pharmaceuticals using graphene [33–44].

Possibly the deadliest illness in the world is cancer. One of the most frequent tumors endangering women is breast cancer [45]. Standard cancer therapies such as surgery, chemotherapy, radiation and hormone therapy are employed. However, tumor growth is also possible after surgery [46]. The estrogen receptor that binds to DNA and controls a variety of biological activities of many genes is an adequate therapeutic choice for patients with breast cancer who are in need of time-limited and non-curative treatments. Antiestrogens are estrogen receptor blockers and slow growth the growth of tumors. Many antiestrogens, including toremifene, have been created as medications that inhibit the estrogen signal [47,48]. TOR is useful for breast cancer treatment, which only varies from tamoxifen in structure only by one chlorine [49]. It has been established that TOR is equally effective as tamoxifen as an adjuvant therapy and for the treatment of metastatic illness in postmenopausal patients [50]. Recently, Ojala et al., reported that NIR spectroscopy had detected the medication dissolving from toremifene tablets [51]. Tamoxifen and toremifene adverse medication responses in breast cancer were compared by Zhou et al., [52]. In this, DFT, AIM and MD analyses were used to evaluate the interaction of the toremifene molecule with carboxylic acid functionalized and N/B-doped graphene.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation is a technique that uses Newton's laws of motion to study the movement of atoms in a molecule. In this case, the simulation was performed using the Gromacs software package, which is widely used and well-established MD simulation software [59]. The method in the simulation was the minimization of the protein–ligand complex in a vacuum. This was done using the steepest descent algorithm, which involves iteratively adjusting the atomic coordinates of the complex in order to minimize the potential energy. After minimization, the complex was solvated in a periodic box of water using the SPC water model. The SPC water model is a simple model that represents water molecules as a single point charge, and is often used as a starting point for more complex water models. The complex was also at a salt concentration of 0.15 M by adding sodium and chloride ions. The resulting complex was then subjected to an NPT (constant pressure, constant temperature) equilibration phase, followed by a production run for 100 ns (nanoseconds) in the NPT ensemble, which is used to simulate systems at constant temperature and pressure, which are commonly encountered in biological systems. Finally, the trajectory of the simulation was analyzed using various tools provided by the Gromacs software package, including RMSD, RMSF, Rg, SASA, and H-Bond. These analyses allow researchers to study the properties of the simulated system, such as its overall shape, flexibility, and interactions with the surrounding solvent [60,61]. For this study, MMPBSA calculations were performed on the complex of TOR. The last 50 ns of Gromacs trajectories for each complex were used for the calculation. First, the complex structures were prepared for the calculation using the Gromacs software, including the addition of explicit solvent and the generation of topology files. The MMPBSA calculation was set up in the g_MMPBSA software [62], and the energy decomposition was performed on each complex over the last 50 ns of the trajectory. The resulting energy components were used to find binding affinity and the contributions of different energy terms to the overall binding energy.

N/B-doped graphene can exhibit different behavior with various doping types and places. Different structures with N/B doping at their centers are presented to investigate doping in drug delivery systems. In pristine nanosurfaces: C-C = 1.37 to 1.43Ǻ (GG), 1.38 to 1.44Ǻ (COOHGG), 1.38 to 1.41Ǻ (BBGG), 1.38 to 1.42Ǻ (NNGG) and C = O = 1.22Ǻ and C-O = 1.36Ǻ in all systems. The bond CB for BBGG is 1.55Ǻ and CN of NNGG is from 1.41 to 1.44Ǻ. The bond length CB for BBGG is 1.55Ǻ and CN of NNGG is from 1.41 to 1.44 Å.

For maximal contact, the TOR is positioned parallel to nanosurfaces at a distance < 3 Å, and thorough optimization is performed. Most stable geometries for complexes are shown in Fig. 3 . TOR-GG has a perpendicular orientation for TOR with GG after adsorption with N atom near to GG at a distance 3.1 Å. In TOR-COOHGG, TOR and COOHGG are nearly parallel at end on position with COOH group and O atom of TOR near to COOH group at distance 1.87 Å from the H atom of COOH and the CH2 group near to O atom TOR is at a distance of 2.46Ǻ from the C = O of COOH. For TOR-BBGG, TOR is nearly perpendicular to BBGG with N atom near to COOH at a distance of 1.71Ǻ and CH2 of TOR near to N atom is at a distance of 2.64Ǻ from C = O of COOH with H atom COOH detached. In TOR-NNGG complex, TOR is nearly perpendicular to NNGG with O of TOR near to NNGG at a distance 1.85Ǻ from COOH and CH2 near to N of TOR is at a distance of 2.47Ǻ for C = O of COOH group (Fig. 3). After TOR adsorption, there is no change for C-C in GG while it becomes 1.37 to 1.43Ǻ in COOHGG, 1.38 to 1.43Ǻ in BBGG, and 1.36 to 1.44 Å in NNGG. The C-N length varies as 1.36 to 1.44Ǻ in NNGG and B-C is 1.54 to 1.56Ǻ in BBGG. The bond lengths of C = O is 1.22Ǻ and around 1.34Ǻ for C-O in all systems after the adsorption of TOR.

To understand the binding locations, MEP maps have been employed to evaluate them (Figs. 1 and 4 ). The hydrogen (blue) and O, N, Cl atoms, and phenyl rings (red and yellow) are the most reactive parts of TOR (Fig. 2). All C of GG, center ring C of COOHGG, B and centre C of BBGG, have wealth of electrons where as for NNGG outer C are yellow and red and are electron rich sites (Fig. 1). In other systems, H are blue with low electron concentrations and the centre portion of NNGG is blue with low electron concentration [63,64]. Also O atom (C = O) is yellow while H (OH) is blue in pristine systems (Fig. 1). The adsorption distances (Fig. 3) demonstrate that interactions are non-covalent [65]. The distribution of reactive sites is reallocated after TOR adsorption, as illustrated in MEP in Fig. 4, illustrative of charge transfer. In TOR-complexes, reactive areas are redistributed: In GG, all C are yellow while O, Cl and phenyl ring red and H blue. Changes are found for distribution of potential in TOR-COOHGG with more red and yellow in COOHGG and with O of C = O red and H of OH blue; In TOR adsorbed BBGG, potential becomes more yellow while O of COOH become more red and in TOR, N atom changes character, more bluish. In TOR adsorbed on NNGG, O of NNGG are red, outer H blue and in TOR phenyl rings and Cl atoms are yellow. From Mulliken's charge analysis, this alteration of reactive zones is caused by chemisorptions and a non-uniform distribution of charges.

Estimates of dipole moment (DM) before and after TOR adsorption are shown in Table 1. The COOHGG and NNGG are polar with low DM of 0.54 and 0.38Debye, while GG and BBGG are nonpolar (zero DM). After the TOR binds to graphene, the DM increases, indicating that complexes are soluble in aqueous medium. All complexes have higher polarizabilities than pure nano surfaces and noticeably higher polarizabilities than the TOR, meaning CT in the complexes with increased NLO activity. Additionally, maximal polarizability for BBGG and NNGG is reported here [67,68].

The energies band gaps are estimated to better comprehend how the nanocarriers and the TOR adsorption of graphene affect each other (Table 3 ). According to measurements, the GG's energy gap is 4.04 eV, in agreement with earlier findings (4.32 eV) [69]. Table 3 shows that all N/B doped complexes have small gaps in comparison with to that of GG so that TOR with GG (3.98 eV) > COOHGG (3.62 eV) > BBGG (1.12 eV) > NNGG (1.02 eV) in air while in water the variations are in the order that in air. The Eg values vary as a result of TOR adsorption: although BBGG/NNGG exhibits a considerable shift (decrease). The adsorption of TOR also affects the energy levels of the FMOs. The DOS spectra of TOR change dramatically after adsorption on the graphene (Fig. S1) [70–72].

The reactivity of the nanocarriers is predicted to be accounted for by chemical descriptors of TOR and their complexes (Table 3). The μ of the nanocarriers is changed when TOR adsorbs on their surfaces. There is also a variation for hardness value during adsorption. Because the electrophilicity index is low (2.31 eV), the TOR is stable. With TOR adsorption, all electrophilicity index values rise both in air and solvent. The simulations show that TOR adsorption on nanocarriers improves the total chemical stability [73]. The FMOs plot of unmodified nanosurfaces and nanosurfaces that have been adsorbed with TOR are shown in Figs. 5 and 6
. For GG, ED are absent along the axial C (HOMO and in LUMO); For COOHGG, HOMO is over the system except COOH and C atom attached with COOH while LUMO is entirely over the system except C atom along the axial line; In BBGG, HOMO is all over the molecule except COOH, C attached to COOH and C atoms in the B attached ring and LUMO is over the center ring containing B atom and C atom attached to COOH group. In NNGG, HOMO is over the entire system except for N and C atoms along N axial line while LUMO is over entire system except, C atoms on N ring, and COOH group (Fig. 5). In adsorbed complexes, HOMO is over the Cl atom, and attached CH2 group for GG and COOHGG while in BBGG, HOMO is over the COOHGG except COOH group and in NNGG, HOMO is over the NNGG system. LUMO is over GG, COOHGG and NNGG except COOH group while it is in Cl atom and attached CH2 group for BBGG (Fig. 6). These electron densities show charge transfer and adsorption.

Mulliken charges of O2, N3 for GG and NNGG, N3 for BBGG, C11 for COOHGG and NNGG, C12 for COOHGG and NNGG, C13 for NNGG, C20 for COOHGG and NNGG, C29 for GG and BBGG systems show changes in values (Table S1). Hydrogen atoms, H42 also show a variation due to chemisorption.

For TOR, TD-DFT gives three absorptions at 255.80, 250.86, and 236.85 nm while for the doped systems the absorptions are mainly: 433.39, 335.43 nm for TOR-GG, 476.17 nm for TOR-COOHGG, 1615.85 nm for TOR-BBGG and 1584.06, 773.64 nm for TOR-NNGG. For the complexes there is shift to higher wavelengths in the visible region; for BBGG and NNGG, absorption occurs above 1000 nm also (Table S2, Fig.S2).

The DFT predicted Raman spectra for the TOR are shown in Fig. S3 both before and after adsorption. A comparison of intensities shows that SERS amplifies the majority of the TOR's vibration modes after the adsorption [74]. In the spectrum of the TOR-nanocarrier complexes, the TOR's inactive Raman modes are also active with increased intensity. Particularly after adsorption, most of the modes of TOR have higher Raman intensities. Maximum enhancement is seen for comparable doped B/N, supporting complexes' chemisorption and adsorption energies.

ce:para.ce:bold: AIM topological analysis and analysis of non-covalent interactions

To obtain the mathematical representations of the electronic structure, electron localization function [82,83] (ELF) analysis was done for the studied systems. ELF (Fig. 8 ) of each structure has V(N) and V'(N) monosynaptic basins associated with the non-bonding electron density of (N) nitrogen atom, giving the population of 5.12 e for GG which is decreased to 3.20 e in COOHGG and NNGG and to 2.93 e in BBGG. The population of the oxygen atom (O1) of the drug molecule associated with V(O1) and V'(O1) of all the four complexes integrate between 5.06 and 5.11 e. Note that, among all the considered structures, negligible changes are observed in any of the disynaptic basins of the drug molecule. Thus, we can conclude that the presence of substituted cages of COOHGG, BBGG and NNGG has a higher tendency to interact with the drug molecule than GG.

To characterize the reactive sites in complex (Fig. 9 ), MEP [84] analysis was performed. The surface minima/maxima are represented in magenta/yellow. The studied MEP isosurface of the GG complex shows 29/40 minima/maxima with ESP values between −55.54 and 37.97 kCalmol−1. Blue zones with negative ESP from −55.54 to −20.47 kCalmol−1 and red zones with positive ESP from 14.59 to 37.97 kCalmol−1 are near the cage as well as the drug, while the maximum area of the isosurface is covered by green zone with ESP from −20.47 to 14.59 kCalmol−1. The MEP of the COOHGG complex gives 27/37 minima/maxima with ESP values between −56.26 and 85.15 kCal mol−1. The isosurface here is covered in the blue and green with negative ESP from −56.26 to −20.91 kCalmol−1 and ESP values between −20.91 and 32.12 kcal mol−1 respectively. The MEP of BBGG complex gives 10/29 minima/maxima with ESP values from −10.31 to 15.87 kCalmol−1. The negative ESP with blue regions from −10.31 to −0.49 kCalmol−1 is located throughout cage, while the drug molecule is located around the green with ESP from −0.49 to 6.05 kCalmol−1 showing red zones with positive ESP between 9.32 and 15.87 kCalmol−1. The NND complex gives 28/39 minima/maxima with ESP between −79.45 and 94.96 kCalmol−1. Herein, the maximum area of the isosurface is in the green zones with ESP between −14.05 kCalmol−1 and 29.56 showing red region with positive ESP value between 51.36 and 94.96 and blue region with a negative ESP value between −79.45 and −14.05 kCalmol−1.

Since TOR has anticancer activity, the epidermal growth factor protein for breast cancer, 1 M17 [85] is selected for docking with Autodock [86] and its binding energy is −6.2 kCal/mol. The interaction of residues with TOR is given in Fig. S4.

The simulation will likely be used to gain insights into how the inhibitor binds to the target protein and how it affects the activity, as well as to predict potential binding sites and inhibitor mechanisms. The bound systems are used to find the dynamic behaviour of 1 M17. (i) protein alone [APO], (ii) p\Protein bound to title compound TOR selected from docking [TOR].

The RMSDs of the APO and TOR protein complexes, measured over a 100 ns period, were found to be 0.311+/-0.035 nm, 0.279+/-0.069 nm, respectively. These values indicate that there was little structural change in the protein complexes during the simulation, and the RMSDs did not deviate significantly from that of the unbound protein (Fig. 10 ). The relative stability of chemical complexes during the course of the simulation is represented by these RMSD values. The RMSD results indicate that all of the four protein complexes considered were relatively stable in the simulation. Small variances between complexes indicate that their stability if quite similar to one another. This is probably because they all have components in common in their structures [87].

RMSF (root mean square fluctuation) analysis is used to identify which amino acids of a protein experience the most movement during a molecular dynamics simulation. This information can be used to understand how the presence or absence of a ligand affects stability of the protein. In this case, RMSF values were determined over a 100 ns simulation timescale, giving an idea of how the protein's vibrations change over time (Fig. 11 ). The outcome indicated that no substantial structural changes occurred during the 100 ns simulation [88].

The mass-weighted average distance of atoms from the protein's center of mass can be used to compute the Rg, which is a measurement of how compact a protein's structure. The Rg plot can be used to visualize how the overall shape and folding of the protein changes over time during a molecular dynamics simulation. The Rg plot in Fig. 12 illustrates the changes in the protein's structure during simulation. Throughout the simulation, APO and TOR complexes showed a similar pattern of Rg. The average Rg (0 to 100 ns) for APO and TOR-1 M17 were 2.01+/-0.014 nm, and 2.04+/-0.019 nm respectively. The radius of gyration is a useful metric for measuring the structural flexibility of the protein complex. The 1 M17 complex's Rg values show that the shapes of the complex folds into at various points along its trajectory are not significantly different. This shows that throughout simulation, 1 M17-TOR remains structurally stable [89].

The SASA changes were examined to find compactness of the hydrophobic core. Fig. 13 depicts the evolution of SASA of the APO and TOR-1 M17 throughout time. The average SASA (0 to 100 ns) for APO and TOR-1 M17 were 156.01+/-4.45 nm, 155.46+/-3.51 nm respectively. This shows that during the simulation, there is no change in the 1 M17′s structural level [90].

The stability of 1 M17-TOR is due to formation of H-bonds between protein and ligand. In this research, the hydrogen bonds that were identified through molecular docking analysis were further confirmed through simulation analysis. H-bond analysis for complexes with TOR is shown in Fig. 14
[91].

We looked at the relative binding strength within the summertime energy protein to ascertain the binding affinity of TOR. The binding strength of TOR in relation to inhibitors, calculated the MM-PBSA technique, is compared. We determine residue-level contributions to the interaction energy throughout a steady simulation trajectory. The total ED and positive LED at the BCPs and RCPs reveal the NCI. The results show that TOR has a van der Waals energy of −147.293+/–22.370 kJ/mol, an electrostatic energy of −53.749+/- 33.825 kJ/mol, a polar solvation energy of 112.531+/-30.651 kJ/mol, and a binding energy of −70.130+/–23.290 kJ/mol. Overall, the TOR had different values for the van der Waals, electrostatic, polar solvation, and binding energies. These differences may be due to various factors, such as the other structures and properties of the TOR. Further analysis would be needed to draw more detailed conclusions about the data [92].

DFT simulations were used to conduct a theoretical research into the adsorption of TOR on coronene surfaces. The TOR adsorption phenomenon was estimated using a variety of characteristics. The most stable complex was discovered to be TOR-BBGG/NNGG, with a binding energy of −55.73/-90.92 kJ/mol in air/water. All complexes have polarizabilities that are higher than those of unaltered nanosurfaces and noticeably higher than those of TOR molecules, demonstrating CT in the complexes with increased NLO activity. Similar B/N doped atoms exhibit intensity augmentation, supporting the complex's chemisorption and adsorption energy values. After adsorption, most of the modes of TOR have higher Raman intensities and maximum enhancement is seen for B/N doped complexes. Since TOR has anticancer activity, docking with 1 M17 gives binding energy of −6.2 kCal/mol. According to MD simulation results, TOR had different values for the van der Waals, electrostatic, polar solvation, and binding energies. The RMSD results show that all of the four protein complexes considered were relatively stable in the simulation. In this case, RMSF values (over a 100 ns) give an idea of how the protein's vibrations change over time and the outcome showed that no substantial structural changes occurred during the simulation. The SASA shows that during the simulation, there is no change in the 1 M17′s structural level.
